communications.subject: Connecticut Freedom of Information Act Request: Connecticut FOIA Request – Town of Fairfield Planning & Zoning Records (2020–Present)
communications.communication:
To Whom It May Concern:

Pursuant to the Connecticut Freedom of Information Act, I hereby request the following records:

Records from January 1, 2020 to present relating to Fairfield Planning & Zoning Department communications and draft materials, including:

1. Communications Involving Planning & Zoning Leadership
Emails to or from Emmeline Harrigan, including eharrigan@fairfieldct.org
Any additional Town email addresses used by Emmeline Harrigan
Any shared or group email accounts to which Emmeline Harrigan had access
Emails involving Jonathan Mullen
Emails involving the Director of Planning & Zoning (current and former)
Emails where the above individuals were sender, recipient, copied, or part of email threads

2. Commission Email Account (tpzcommission@fairfieldct.org)
Emails received by tpzcommission@fairfieldct.org
Emails forwarded to or from tpzcommission@fairfieldct.org
Internal communications discussing emails received by this account
Summaries, digests, or compilations of emails sent to the commission
Records reflecting how emails sent to tpzcommission@fairfieldct.org
 were reviewed, filtered, or distributed
Policies, procedures, or informal practices governing handling of this account

3. Draft Staff Reports and Internal Analyses
Draft staff reports
Draft memoranda or internal analyses
Tracked-changes or redlined versions
Email discussions regarding drafts or changes to recommendations
Draft calculations or revised analyses

4. Communications With Applicants and Developers
Emails between Planning & Zoning staff and applicants or property owners
Emails with developers or project sponsors
Emails with attorneys representing applicants or developers
Emails with engineers, architects, or consultants representing applicants
Pre-application discussions, informal guidance, or revisions to proposals
Communications with attorneys representing applicants or developers are included, as these involve external parties and are generally not subject to attorney-client privilege. This request does not seek privileged communications solely between the Town and its own legal counsel.

5. Consultant Communications
Emails with planning consultants
Emails with housing consultants
Emails with GIS consultants
Emails with traffic consultants
Emails with other land-use consultants
Drafts, analyses, or deliverables prepared by consultants

6. GIS and Calculation-Related Records
Emails and draft documents relating to GIS analyses
Zoning or density calculations
Affordable housing or 8-30g calculations
Protest petition calculations
Methodology discussions or revisions

7. Text Messages and Messaging Applications
Text messages, SMS, iMessage, or messaging application communications relating to the categories above
Messages to or from Emmeline Harrigan relating to Planning & Zoning matters
Messages involving tpzcommission@fairfieldct.org communications or distribution decisions
Messages involving draft staff reports, calculations, or communications with applicants, developers, or consultants

This request includes communications conducted on Town-issued devices, personal devices, personal email accounts, or messaging applications where such devices or accounts were used for Town business.

To assist in processing, responsive records may include those containing terms such as “draft,” “calculation,” “GIS,” “8-30g,” “affordable housing,” “consultant,” “staff report,” “application,” or “methodology.”

Electronic searches of email systems, shared mailboxes, and messaging platforms are acceptable. If any portion of this request is considered overly broad or burdensome, I am willing to work cooperatively to narrow the request.

I also request that, if possible and pursuant to Conn. Gen. Stat. § 1-212(d), fees be waived as I believe this request is in the public interest and not made for commercial gain. The requested documents will be made available to the public at MuckRock.com.

In the event that there are fees, I would be grateful if you would inform me of the total charges in advance of fulfilling my request. I would prefer the request filled electronically, by e-mail attachment if available or CD-ROM if not.

Thank you in advance for your anticipated cooperation in this matter. I look forward to receiving your response to this request within 4 business days, as the statute requires.

Sincerely,

FairfieldResident

title: Denver-area attempted armored car robbery (July 20, 1993)

communications.communication:
To Whom It May Concern:

Pursuant to the Freedom of Information Act, I hereby request the following records:

Under the Freedom of Information Act, 5 U.S.C. § 552, I request access to all records concerning the armed ambush and attempted robbery of an armored car guard outside a Denver, Colorado bank on July 20, 1993, and any related federal investigation or retrospective case-linkage analysis connecting that incident to Larry Eugene Phillips Jr. and Emil Dechebal Matasareanu.
Incident Description
On or about July 20, 1993, an armored car guard was ambushed outside a Denver, Colorado bank by two armed suspects who used military assault-style weapons, believed to be AK-47-pattern rifles, and who approached the guard from two directions as he was leaving the bank with money. The exact bank branch address is not known to me; I request that the FBI search for the incident by date, method, and Denver field office case numbers rather than by address alone.
On March 8, 1997, the Los Angeles Times reported that William Rehder, the coordinator of bank robbery investigations for the FBI’s Los Angeles field office, confirmed that federal authorities were working in conjunction with the FBI’s Denver office to examine the July 20, 1993 incident and to determine whether it was the first in a series of robberies committed by Larry Eugene Phillips Jr. (DOB 09/01/1970, deceased 02/28/1997) and Emil Dechebal Matasareanu (DOB 07/19/1966, deceased 02/28/1997). Rehder is quoted as stating that the physical descriptions of the suspects and the use of military assault-style weapons in the Denver incident resembled the later Los Angeles-area robberies, and that if the attribution held, the July 20, 1993 attack was “where this kind of activity started.” (Beth Shuster, “Gunmen May Be Linked to Denver Attack,” Los Angeles Times, March 8, 1997, p. B1.)
The same article noted that federal authorities were separately investigating whether Phillips and Matasareanu were responsible for the June 14, 1995 homicide of Brink’s courier Herman Dwight Cook outside a Winnetka, California bank, an incident believed by the FBI to share methodological similarities with the Denver ambush.
Subjects of the Request
— Larry Eugene Phillips Jr. (DOB September 1, 1970, deceased February 28, 1997)
— Emil Dechebal Matasareanu (DOB July 19, 1966, deceased February 28, 1997)
— The July 20, 1993 armored car guard ambush in Denver, Colorado (victim and bank branch unknown to requester; FBI Denver field office case number unknown)
— The FBI’s retrospective case-linkage analysis conducted in March 1997 following the February 28, 1997 North Hollywood shootout
Records Requested
I request the following records:
1. The complete FBI Denver field office case file concerning the July 20, 1993 armored car guard ambush outside a Denver bank, including the opening memoranda, investigative reports (FD-302s), witness statements, ballistic and forensic analysis, crime scene documentation, and any closing memoranda.
2. Any FBI Los Angeles field office working file, analytical memoranda, or correspondence generated in March 1997 or thereafter in connection with the retrospective examination of the July 20, 1993 Denver incident for case linkage to Phillips and Matasareanu, including any reports, memoranda, or teletypes authored by or routed through Special Agent William Rehder or the FBI LA bank robbery unit.
3. All inter-field-office correspondence between the FBI Los Angeles Field Office and the FBI Denver Field Office concerning Phillips, Matasareanu, the July 20, 1993 Denver incident, or the broader “High Incident Bandits” investigation, during the period March 1, 1997 through December 31, 1998.
4. Any FBI correspondence with the Denver Police Department concerning the July 20, 1993 incident, including any communications with Detective Jim Hess or any other assigned Denver PD investigators. Note: Detective Jim Hess is identified by the March 8, 1997 Los Angeles Times article as having discussed the Phillips 1992 Denver real estate fraud case with the press in the immediate aftermath of the North Hollywood shootout.
5. Any FBI case-linkage memoranda comparing the July 20, 1993 Denver incident to the June 14, 1995 homicide of Brink’s courier Herman Dwight Cook in Winnetka, California, and to any other armored car attacks or attempted armored car attacks during the period 1993–1996 in which Phillips and Matasareanu were considered as possible suspects.
6. Any FBI analytical or intelligence products generated under the “High Incident Bandits” designation that reference the July 20, 1993 Denver incident, whether generated before or after the February 28, 1997 North Hollywood shootout.
7. Any FBI correspondence with the Brink’s Company, Loomis, Wells Fargo Armored Service, or any other armored car company concerning the July 20, 1993 Denver incident, including incident reports, route records, and insurance claim documentation that may have been shared with federal investigators.
8. Any final determinations, closing memoranda, or analytical conclusions reached by the FBI concerning whether the July 20, 1993 Denver incident was or was not attributable to Phillips and Matasareanu, and the evidentiary basis for that determination.
Fee Waiver and News Media Requester Classification
I request a fee waiver under 5 U.S.C. § 552(a)(4)(A)(iii) or, in the alternative, news media requester classification under 5 U.S.C. § 552(a)(4)(A)(ii)(II).
I am an independent author writing a book-length work of narrative nonfiction on the February 28, 1997 North Hollywood shootout and its context, including the criminal trajectory of Phillips and Matasareanu during the period 1992–1997. The manuscript is in active development and is intended for national publication and general readership.
The requested records concern identifiable federal government law enforcement operations of documented public interest. Disclosure would contribute significantly to public understanding of the federal investigative response to a series of armed robberies that culminated in the largest urban gun battle in modern American law enforcement history. The July 20, 1993 Denver incident is of particular significance because, if the FBI’s retrospective attribution was confirmed, it would represent the earliest known violent crime in the series, predating the previously documented robberies by nearly three years and substantially altering the chronology of the Phillips-Matasareanu criminal partnership as it has been reported in the secondary literature. The FBI’s investigative file is the only source that could resolve whether the attribution was confirmed, disproved, or left open.
I qualify as a representative of the news media under the statutory definition: I gather information of potential interest to a segment of the public, use editorial skills to turn raw materials into a distinct work, and intend to distribute that work to a national audience through established trade publication channels. Any advance or royalties I may receive represent standard compensation for journalistic and historical labor, not a disqualifying commercial interest.
I note that I have a separate FOIPA request pending before the FBI (Request No. 1730549-000) concerning the broader “High Incident Bandits” investigation, and that I am currently appealing the denial of the fee waiver on that request to the Office of Information Policy. The present request is separate and distinct, targets a different predicate incident and a different FBI field office, and should be processed independently.
Fee Authorization
If the fee waiver is denied, I authorize fees up to $75.00. Please contact me before incurring any costs exceeding that amount so I may either authorize additional fees or further narrow the scope.

The requested documents will be made available to the general public, and this request is not being made for commercial purposes.

In the event that there are fees, I would be grateful if you would inform me of the total charges in advance of fulfilling my request. I would prefer the request filled electronically, by e-mail attachment if available or CD-ROM if not.

Thank you in advance for your anticipated cooperation in this matter. I look forward to receiving your response to this request within 20 business days, as the statute requires.

Sincerely,

Daniel Brown

title: George W. Bush Library: WHTV Tapes of November 13th 2001 Bush-Putin Oval Office Meeting (aka: "pool spray")

communications.subject: Freedom of Information Act Request: George W. Bush Library: WHTV Tapes of November 13th 2001 Bush-Putin Oval Office Meeting (aka: "pool spray")
communications.communication:
To Whom It May Concern:

Pursuant to the Freedom of Information Act, I hereby request the following records:

I am seeking access to and, if possible, copies of audiovisual records documenting the Oval Office “pool spray” featuring President George W. Bush and President Vladimir Putin on November 13, 2001.

Specifically, I request:

Any and all video recordings created or retained by the White House Communications Agency (WHCA), including WHTV (White House Television) footage, of the Oval Office pool spray involving Presidents Bush and Putin on November 13, 2001.
Any associated raw, unedited, or “b-roll” footage from that same event.
Any available shot logs, tape logs, metadata records, or catalog entries associated with this footage.
Any derivative clips or internally distributed versions of the same recording.

To assist with identification, the requested material may be described or indexed under terms such as:

“pool spray”
“Oval Office”
“Bush Putin meeting”
“11/13/2001”

title: Woodland Hills armored vehicle attack (March 27, 1996)

communications.subject: Freedom of Information Act Request: Woodland Hills armored vehicle attack (March 27, 1996)
communications.communication:
To Whom It May Concern:

Pursuant to the Freedom of Information Act, I hereby request the following records:

Under the Freedom of Information Act, 5 U.S.C. § 552, I request access to all records concerning the armed attack on Brink’s armored van #68304 on Fallbrook Avenue, Woodland Hills, California, on or about March 27, 1996.
Incident Description
On or about March 27, 1996, armed men attacked a Brink’s armored van (vehicle identifier #68304) in the vicinity of Fallbrook Avenue, Woodland Hills, California. The incident is believed by some investigators to have been committed by Larry Eugene Phillips Jr. (DOB 09/01/1970, deceased 02/28/1997) and Emil Dechebal Matasareanu (DOB 07/19/1966, deceased 02/28/1997), and has been tentatively linked to the “High Incident Bandits” federal investigation concluded after the February 28, 1997 North Hollywood shootout. The attribution to Phillips and Matasareanu has been characterized by researchers as “tenuous,” and federal records would clarify whether and how the FBI attributed this incident.
Records Requested
I request the following records related to the March 27, 1996 Woodland Hills Brink’s incident:
1. The FBI case file, including opening memoranda, investigative reports, and any closing memoranda;
2. Witness statements from Brink’s personnel, civilians, or law enforcement officers present at the scene;
3. Any forensic or ballistic reports concerning evidence recovered from the scene;
4. Case-linkage memoranda connecting this incident to the “High Incident Bandits” investigation or to other armored car attacks in the Los Angeles area during 1995–1996;
5. Interagency correspondence between the FBI, the Los Angeles Police Department, Brink’s corporate security, and other agencies concerning this incident;
6. Any post-February 28, 1997 memoranda retroactively attributing this incident to Phillips and Matasareanu, or memoranda declining to make such an attribution;
7. Any records cross-referencing this incident to the June 14, 1995 homicide of Brink’s courier Herman Dwight Cook in Winnetka, California, which is the subject of a separate FOIA request.
Fee Waiver and News Media Requester Classification
I request a fee waiver under 5 U.S.C. § 552(a)(4)(A)(iii) or, in the alternative, news media requester classification under 5 U.S.C. § 552(a)(4)(A)(ii)(II).
I am an independent author writing a book-length work of narrative nonfiction on the “High Incident Bandits” case, the February 28, 1997 North Hollywood shootout, and its institutional and policy consequences. The manuscript is in active development and is intended for national publication and general readership. The requested records concern identifiable federal government law enforcement operations of documented public interest, and disclosure would contribute significantly to public understanding of the federal investigative response to a series of armed robberies that culminated in the largest urban gun battle in modern American law enforcement history. The March 27, 1996 Woodland Hills incident is of particular significance because the attribution of this attack to Phillips and Matasareanu is contested in the secondary literature, and the federal record is the only source that could definitively resolve the question.

On or about May 31, 1996, two armed men wearing body armor entered the Bank of America branch at 20118 Roscoe Boulevard, Winnetka, California, and committed an armed takeover robbery. title: Clinton Presidential Library: Project Impact Transition & Budgetary Codification (Nov 2000 – Jan 2001)

communications.subject: Freedom of Information Act Request: Clinton Presidential Library: Project Impact Transition & Budgetary Codification (Nov 2000 – Jan 2001)
communications.communication:
To Whom It May Concern:

Pursuant to the Freedom of Information Act, I hereby request the following records:

Pursuant to the Freedom of Information Act (5 U.S.C. § 552) and the Presidential Records Act (44 U.S.C. §§ 2201-2207), I hereby request access to and copies of all records created or received by the Clinton Administration between November 7, 2000, and January 20, 2001, regarding the FEMA initiative "Project Impact: Building Disaster Resistant Communities."

To facilitate a comprehensive search of the Library’s holdings, I specifically request that the following systems and classifications be queried:

1. Automated Records Management System (ARMS) Search
I request a keyword search of the ARMS email database for all correspondence sent to or from FEMA Director James Lee Witt, the White House Office of Cabinet Affairs, and the Office of Management and Budget (OMB) containing the following terms:

"Project Impact" AND "FY2002"

"Project Impact" AND "Passback"

"Project Impact" AND "Transition"

"Project Impact" AND "Codification" or "Legislative mandate"

2. WHORM Subject File Search
I request a review of the White House Office of Records Management (WHORM) Subject Files, with particular emphasis on the following codes:

FG385 (Federal Emergency Management Agency): All folders related to end-of-term "Action Memos" or "Briefing Books" discussing the expansion of Project Impact.

FI (Finance): Specifically records regarding the FY2002 Budget Passback and "placeholder" funding levels for FEMA’s Directorate of Mitigation.

ST047 (Washington State): Any correspondence regarding the designation of new "Project Impact Communities" in the Pacific Northwest during the lame-duck period.

3. Specific Record Types Requested
This request includes, but is not limited to:

Budget Passback Documents: All OMB documents detailing the proposed funding levels for Project Impact for the 2002 Fiscal Year provided to the incoming Bush-Cheney transition team.

"Lock-in" Proposals: Policy memos discussing the potential to "codify" Project Impact through executive order or legislative language before January 20, 2001.

New Designations: A list of all municipalities or regions officially designated or "provisionally approved" as Project Impact Communities between Election Day 2000 and the Inauguration.

Format of Records:
I request that these records be provided in their original electronic format where available, particularly for records retrieved from the ARMS database, to preserve metadata and threading.

If certain parts of this request or if certain portions must be redacted, please provide any reasonable segregable portion of a requested record after removing or redacting those portions claimed to be exempt, explain in writing the justification for redacting the record, indicate the extent of any redactions on the portion of the record which is made available or published and where technically feasible indicate on the redacted portion itself the specific exemption(s) claimed.

I am interested in the federal government's response to the 2001 Nisqually earthquake, and the release of this information will provide the public with useful historical context. 

Thank you for the commitment to transparency, and I look forward to having this request resolved.

The requested documents will be made available to the general public, and this request is not being made for commercial purposes.

Robert Delaware

title: Records, Index Entries, and Metadata Relating to Shapoor Reporter, Ardeshir Reporter, and Iran (1950–1960) (Central Intelligence Agency)

communications.communication:
To Whom It May Concern:

Pursuant to the Freedom of Information Act, I hereby request the following records:

Sir Shapoor Ardeshirji Reporter (1921–2013), including but not limited to the variants “Shapoor Reporter,” “Shapour Reporter,” “Sir Shapoor Reporter,” “Sir Shapour Reporter,” “S. Reporter,” and “S. A. Reporter.”

This request is limited to the period from 1 January 1950 through 31 December 1960.

This request seeks records in which this individual is named, referenced, indexed, described, or assessed, whether as a principal subject or as an incidental contact, intermediary, liaison, introducer, adviser, or participant in diplomatic, intelligence, or political activity relating to Iran.

To ensure clarity and completeness, I am requesting the following categories of records:

First, substantive records. This includes intelligence reports, cables, memoranda, diplomatic correspondence, briefing papers, analytical products, situation reports, and internal summaries that mention or refer to Shapoor Reporter.

Second, communications and liaison records. This includes records of contact, meetings, coordination, or exchanges involving U.S., British, or Iranian officials in which Shapoor Reporter is referenced.

Third, administrative and reference records. This includes file jackets, routing slips, registry entries, cross-reference sheets, dissemination records, and other materials that identify or describe records concerning Shapoor Reporter.

Fourth, index and metadata records. This includes name traces, index entries, database search results, file titles, file numbers, date ranges, originating offices, and any catalog or archival metadata sufficient to identify the existence and structure of responsive records, even where underlying documents are withheld.

Fifth, contextual records. This includes records in which Shapoor Reporter is referenced in connection with one or more of the following: Mohammad Reza Pahlavi, the Shah of Iran; Iranian court politics; Anglo-American coordination in Iran; British diplomatic or intelligence personnel; and political developments surrounding the 1953 coup.

If responsive records are withheld in whole or in part, I request that your agency provide, to the maximum extent permitted by law, the file title, file number or identifier, date or date range, originating office, and a brief description sufficient to understand the nature of the record. I also request that all reasonably segregable non-exempt material be released and that any exemptions be specifically identified.

If any responsive records have been transferred to or are held by another agency or by the National Archives and Records Administration, I request that this request be referred or that you provide the relevant transfer, accession, or file reference information.

If this request is deemed too broad, please process it in the following order of priority:

1. Index entries, name traces, and metadata (1950–1960)  
2. Records explicitly naming Shapoor Reporter (1950–1960)  
3. Records linking Shapoor Reporter to Iran, the Shah, or British liaison activity  


Thank you for your consideration.

The requested documents will be made available to the general public, and this request is not being made for commercial purposes.

In the event that there are fees, I would be grateful if you would inform me of the total charges in advance of fulfilling my request. I would prefer the request filled electronically, by e-mail attachment if available or CD-ROM if not.

Thank you in advance for your anticipated cooperation in this matter. I look forward to receiving your response to this request within 20 business days, as the statute requires.

Sincerely,

Adil Minocherhomjee

title: FBI RB 4.6.26

communications.communication:
Dear FOIA Officer:

You are receiving this correspondence as a request under the Freedom of Information Act, 5 U.S.C. subsection 552. I am requesting the following:

Copies of all Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI) records, documents, and communications responsive to Raymond Augustine Bauer, aka Raymond A. Bauer, aka Raymond Bauer (September 7, 1916-July 10, 1977; SSN 321-05-3494 ).

Dr. Bauer was a professor of the behavioral sciences at Harvard University and holder of the Joseph C. Wilson professorship at the Harvard School of Business Administration. Dr. Bauer was among the first of the Cold War Sovietologists, writing extensively on the social structure and psychology of the Soviet Union in books like "The New Man in Soviet Psychology" and "How the Soviet System Works".

It is public knowledge that the FBI has a file on Dr. Bauer: the historian David C. Engerman identified its existence in his 2009 book "Know Your Enemy: The Rise and Fall of America's Soviet Experts". Writing on page 58, he states: "Yet the very source of this friend’s recollection indicates the problem at hand: it is among the nearly 200 pages of Bauer’s FBI file. Bauer faced a major security inquisition in 1954, forcing him to abandon the prospect of doing the kind of classified consulting that [Clyde] Kluckhohn and [Alex] Inkeles did. He defended his college record by telling his boss that he joined the [American Student Union] in order to fight Communist control, but at the same time worried that 'a careless reader might interpret' an article in the student newspaper 'as a ‘Party-line’ argument.' After leaving Northwestern, Bauer joined the U.S. Navy and was soon sent to the navy’s Oriental Languages School in Colorado. His navy experience, too, concerned security investigators; he had been friendly with a classmate, William Remington, who was later convicted of perjury for lying about his membership in the Communist Party. Such a friendship was not a crime even in those years, but added to suspicions about Bauer. As if the ASU and a brush with an actual Communist were not enough of a problem for the FBI, Bauer joined the American Veterans Committee at Harvard just after the war, staying with the group until its collapse."

The FBI's FOIA logs confirm Dr. Engerman filed FOIPA request 982461-000 on 8/21/03, and was granted a partial release on 4/30/04. 

This file was also requested by communications historian Christopher Simpson on 10/4/89, via FOIPA request 321078-D00, according to the FBI's FOIA logs. Although this request was administratively closed on 4/24/90 because it was a duplicate request, it demonstrates ongoing interest in Dr. Bauer as a subject of public interest.

Proof of death is not required as Dr. Bauer's date of birth was over 100 years ago; however, Dr. Bauer's obituary appeared in the New York Times on July 11, 1977. A copy can be found here: https://www.nytimes.com/1977/07/11/archives/raymond-bauer-60-business-professor-taught-at-harvard.html

I am asking you waive any fees associated with this request. I am a member of the news media. My bylines, which include Unicorn Riot, Covert Action Magazine, and the Geopolitical Economy Report, can be found in both print and electronic publications, and I am sending this request in coordination with Muckrock, an award-winning and nationally-recognized news organization. Please take note of the Office of Management and Budget guidelines published March 27, 1987 (52 FR 10012) that include electronic publications and other nontraditional publishers as representatives of the news media.

A fee waiver is appropriate in situations where "disclosure of the requested information is in the public interest because it is likely to contribute significantly to public understanding of the operations and activities of the government, and is not primarily in the commercial interest of the requester,” 5 U.S.C. § 552(a)(4)(iii). At various points in time, Dr. Bauer worked overtly or covertly for the government, conducting Cold War era social science research. He held a fellowship at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology's Center for International Studies (CENIS) while writing "The New Man in Soviet Psychology"; CENIS was covertly funded by the Central Intelligence Agency, according to journalists David Wise and Thomas Ross in their 1965 book "The Invisible Government." Any FBI records relevant to Dr. Bauer would contribute significantly to public understanding of how the FBI handled security investigations into academics with ties to the military and intelligence community during the Cold War. Per Dr. Engerman, the FBI's investigation reviewed alleged ties to communism, and given Dr. Bauer's role as one of the foremost Sovietologists of the early 1950s, any investigation would be of public interest.

Additionally, these records have already been released on at least one occasion, to Dr. Engerman, so there should be no issue with releasing them to other parties.

This request is not being made for commercial purposes. All documents will be provided to the general public without charge.

Please conduct a search of the Central Records System, including but not limited to the Electronic Surveillance (ELSUR) Records System, the Microphone Surveillance (MISUR) Indices, the Physical Surveillance (FISUR) Indices, and the Technical Surveillance (TESUR) Indices, for both main-file records and cross-reference records related to the Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Center for International Studies, United States Air Force, Human Resources Research Institute, and Harvard University.

In the event that there are fees, I am willing to pay up to $100. Please advise if fees will exceed this amount so I might make an arrangement for requesting the records. I would prefer the request filled electronically, by e-mail attachment if available or CD-ROM if not.

I look forward to your response within 20 working days, as the statute requires. If access to the records I am requesting will take longer, please contact me with information about when I might expect copies or the ability to inspect the requested records.

If you deny any or all of this request, please cite each specific exemption you feel justifies the refusal to release the information and notify me of the appeal procedures available to me under the law.

Sincerely,

Robert Skvarla, Jr.

title: California Public Records Act Request — Riverside County, CA. Body-Worn Camera Footage, Officer-Involved Shootings, July 1st-31st 2025

communications.communication:
To Whom It May Concern:

Pursuant to the California Public Records Act, I hereby request the following records:

I hereby formally request access to and copies of the following public records maintained by the Riverside County Sheriff's Department:

All body-worn camera (BWC) video and audio recordings from any and all deputies, officers, or law enforcement personnel involved in or present at any officer-involved shooting (OIS) incident that occurred within Riverside County Sheriff's Department jurisdiction between July 1st 2025 and July 31st 2025, inclusive.

I am requesting the following records for each qualifying officer-involved shooting incident occurring within the stated date range:

1. All body-worn camera video footage from deputies who discharged a firearm during the incident.
2. All body-worn camera video footage from any deputies who were present on scene during or immediately following the discharge of a firearm, including assisting and backup officers.
3. All body-worn camera video footage capturing the period immediately preceding, during, and following each incident, including approach, engagement, and post-incident medical response.
4. All corresponding dashcam or in-car video footage from patrol vehicles present at the scene of each incident.
5. All related CAD (Computer-Aided Dispatch) audio and dispatch recordings pertaining to each incident.
6. All incident or crime reports directly associated with each qualifying officer-involved shooting, to the extent disclosable under SB 1421 and existing CPRA law.

Legal Basis for Disclosure
This request is supported by multiple provisions of California law that specifically mandate the disclosure of officer-involved shooting records:

Senate Bill 1421 (Penal Code § 832.7(b)(1)(A)) requires disclosure of records relating to incidents in which a peace officer discharged a firearm at a person, regardless of whether the shooting resulted in death or injury.
Assembly Bill 748 (Gov. Code § 6254(f)(4)) requires law enforcement agencies to release video and audio recordings of "critical incidents," defined to include any incident in which an officer discharges a firearm at a person, within 45 days of the incident, unless the agency can demonstrate by clear and convincing evidence that disclosure would substantially interfere with an active criminal or administrative investigation.
The California Supreme Court has held that agencies may not impose unreasonable fees or use cost as a barrier to the disclosure of records mandated under SB 1421 and AB 748 (City of Eureka v. Superior Court, 2020).

Should any portion of a requested record be withheld, I respectfully request that you comply with Gov. Code § 7922.000 and provide a written explanation specifying the precise legal exemption or privilege claimed for each withheld item, as well as a detailed description of the nature of the record being withheld sufficient to assess the applicability of the claimed exemption.

Format and Delivery
I request that all responsive records be produced in their original, unaltered digital format (e.g., MP4, AVI, or the native format used by the department's body-worn camera system).

The requested documents will be made available to the general public, and this request is not being made for commercial purposes.

In the event that there are fees, I would be grateful if you would inform me of the total charges in advance of fulfilling my request. I would prefer the request filled electronically, by e-mail attachment if available or CD-ROM if not.

Thank you in advance for your anticipated cooperation in this matter. I look forward to receiving your response to this request within 10 calendar days, as the statute requires.

Sincerely,

James Preston

title: Florida Sunshine Law Request — Pinellas County, FL. Body-Worn Camera Footage, Officer-Involved Shootings, July 1, 2024 – December 31, 2024

communications.communication:
To Whom It May Concern:

Pursuant to the Florida Sunshine Law, I hereby request the following records:

All body-worn camera (BWC) video and audio recordings from any and all deputies or law enforcement personnel involved in or present at any officer-involved shooting (OIS) or deputy-involved shooting (DIS) incident that occurred within Pinellas County Sheriff's Office jurisdiction between July 1, 2024 and December 31, 2024, inclusive.

Specific Records Requested
For each qualifying incident within the stated date range, I am requesting:

1. All body-worn camera video footage from deputies who discharged a firearm during the incident.
2. All body-worn camera video footage from any deputies present on scene during or immediately following the discharge of a firearm, including assisting, backup, and responding officers.
3. All body-worn camera footage capturing the period immediately preceding, during, and following each incident — including initial approach, commands given, engagement, and post-incident medical response.
4. All corresponding dashcam or in-car video footage from patrol vehicles present at or responding to the scene.
5. All Computer-Aided Dispatch (CAD) logs and dispatch audio recordings associated with each qualifying incident.
6. All available incident reports, offense reports, or narrative summaries associated with each qualifying OIS incident, to the extent not exempt under § 119.071, F.S.
7. The full name, badge number, and years of service of involved deputy/deputies, to the extent disclosable under Florida law.
8. Any press release, official statement, or public communication issued by the Pinellas County Sheriff's Office in connection with each qualifying incident.

Legal Basis
Under Chapter 119.07(1)(a), Florida Statutes, every person has the right to inspect and copy public records. Law enforcement recordings and incident documentation created in the course of official duties are public records under Florida law. Florida maintains a strong presumption in favor of public access, and any exemptions must be narrowly construed.

Should any portion of the requested records be withheld, I respectfully request that you identify each document withheld, cite the specific statutory exemption relied upon for each withheld item, and provide all non-exempt, reasonably segregable portions of any partially exempt records pursuant to § 119.07(1)(d), F.S.

Format and Delivery
I request all responsive records be produced in their original, unaltered digital format (e.g., MP4, AVI, or the native format used by the department's body-worn camera system).

The requested documents will be made available to the general public, and this request is not being made for commercial purposes.

title: CalGang (San Diego County Sheriff's Department)

communications.subject: California Public Records Act Request: CalGang (San Diego County Sheriff's Department)
communications.communication:
To Whom It May Concern:

Pursuant to the California Public Records Act, I hereby request the following records:

Hello, 

A memoradnum of understanding is required for outside agencies to access CalGang databases. I am requesting the MOUs between this department and ICE and Customs and Border Protection (CBP). I am requesting copies of those requests from 2016 to today. Along with the information below, for the same time frames. 

An Agency that is interested in accessing the CalGang database shall submit a written
request to the Department or the local Node Administrator on the Agency’s letterhead. The
written request shall provide the following:
(1) A reason for its need to access the CalGang database.
(A) The Agency shall demonstrate a need to know and a right to know to satisfy
subdivision (e)(1).
(2) The number of Users who will be accessing the CalGang database at that Agency and
the level of access that is requested for each User.
(3) The Agency’s assigned originating agency identifier (ORI) number, if applicable.
(4) The addresses of the facilities within the Agency where Users will access the
CalGang database.
(5) The name and contact information for the person(s) designated by the Agency as the
point of contact and training coordinator. If the Agency’s point of contact and training
coordinator are two different persons, the Agency shall identify both.
(6) If data entry access is requested, the Agency shall identify the classification(s),
rank(s), and related expertise of the person(s) who will be responsible for performing the
supervisory review required by sections 752.8 and 753.4; and describe the organizational
structure and process through which the supervisory review process required by sections
752.8 and 753.4 will occur.


Thank you

The requested documents will be made available to the general public, and this request is not being made for commercial purposes.

In the event that there are fees, I would be grateful if you would inform me of the total charges in advance of fulfilling my request. I would prefer the request filled electronically, by e-mail attachment if available or CD-ROM if not.

Thank you in advance for your anticipated cooperation in this matter. I look forward to receiving your response to this request within 10 calendar days, as the statute requires.

Sincerely,

Joey Scott
